Vulnerability Alert: Linux “pedit COW”  CVE-2026-46331 is a Linux kernel flaw that could allow a local unprivileged user to gain root access on affected systems.  A public exploit is available and may poison cached binaries in memory without changing files on disk, meaning file-integrity checks may not catch it.  Prioritize patching multi-tenant hosts, CI/CD runners, Kubernetes nodes, build workers, and shared systems.  Patch kernels. Reboot. Validate exposure.  #Cybersecurity #LinuxSecurity #VulnerabilityAlert #CVE202646331 #KernelSecurity #PatchManagement #CloneSystems

Microsoft 365 AppsのExcelに、悪意あるスプレッドシートを開くだけでログイン中ユーザー権限の任意コード実行につながる脆弱性CVE-2025-60727が公表され、企業環境で懸念が広がっている。  SentinelOneの報告によると、CVE-2025-60727はExcelのファイル解析処理における境界外読み取り（CWE-125）に起因する。細工された.xlsや.xlsxを処理する際、長さやオフセット値の検証が不十分なため、バッファ外のメモリを読み取らされる可能性がある。  影響を受ける製品には、x86とx64のMicrosoft 365 Apps、Excel 2016、Office 2019、Office LTSC 2021/2024、Office Online Serverが含まれる。悪用には通常、被害者が悪性ファイルを開く必要があり、配布経路としてフィッシングメール、ファイル共有サービス、ドライブバイダウンロードが挙げられている。  悪用に成功すると、攻撃者は被害者と同じ権限でコードを実行できる。記事執筆時点で実悪用や公開PoCは確認されていない。監視対象として、Excelによるcmd.exeやPowerShellなどの起動、EXCEL.EXEからの不審な外部通信、異常なOLEオブジェクトを含む文書、解析時のクラッシュが示された。Microsoftは修正更新を公開済みだ。  gbhackers.com/microsoft-365-…

sample_tweets.text: 𝗦𝗲𝗰𝘂𝗿𝗶𝘁𝘆: 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗙𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗢𝗳 𝗕𝗶𝘁𝗧𝗼𝗿𝗿𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗔𝗻𝗱 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗙𝘂𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗢𝗳 𝗪𝗲𝗯𝟯  When people talk about BitTorrent, they often focus on speed, peer-to-peer file sharing, or decentralized storage.  But one of its most important contributions is often overlooked:  Security through decentralization.  For over two decades, BitTorrent has demonstrated that distributing data across a global network can create systems that are not only scalable, but also resilient. That same principle has become one of the cornerstones of Web3, where security is built into the architecture rather than relying on a single trusted intermediary.  𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝗦𝗲𝗰𝘂𝗿𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗠𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘀  Every digital interaction depends on trust.  Whether you’re transferring funds, storing files, running a decentralized application, or accessing digital assets, users need confidence that:  • their data hasn’t been altered  • files remain available  • transactions execute as intended  • infrastructure continues operating even when parts of the network fail  Traditional internet services typically achieve this by placing trust in centralized providers.  If that provider experiences an outage, cyberattack, censorship, or hardware failure, users may lose access to services or data.  BitTorrent approaches the challenge differently.  𝗦𝗲𝗰𝘂𝗿𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗧𝗵𝗿𝗼𝘂𝗴𝗵 𝗗𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗯𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻  Instead of storing or distributing files from a single server, BitTorrent divides files into smaller pieces that are shared across many independent peers.  Each participant contributes bandwidth and helps distribute the content.  This architecture offers several important advantages.  ✓ No Single Point of Failure  In centralized systems, one server becoming unavailable can interrupt access.  In BitTorrent, multiple peers can continue sharing the same file even if individual devices disconnect.  The network doesn’t rely on one machine remaining online.  ✓ Improved Availability  As more peers participate, additional sources become available for downloading.  Rather than creating bottlenecks, increased participation can improve overall distribution efficiency.  The community itself strengthens the network.  ✓ Built-In Resilience  Because files exist across many participants, the network can tolerate individual failures without stopping distribution.  This makes decentralized systems inherently more fault tolerant than architectures built around a single location.  ✓ Data Integrity  BitTorrent verifies downloaded pieces using cryptographic hashes.  Each piece of a file is checked against its expected hash before being accepted.  If a piece has been corrupted or modified, it fails verification and is discarded.  This helps ensure that reconstructed files match their original contents.  𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗦𝗮𝗺𝗲 𝗣𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗰𝗶𝗽𝗹𝗲𝘀 𝗣𝗼𝘄𝗲𝗿 𝗪𝗲𝗯𝟯  Many of the ideas now associated with blockchain ecosystems were demonstrated by peer-to-peer networking long before cryptocurrencies became mainstream.  Web3 extends these concepts beyond file sharing.  Instead of decentralizing only data distribution, blockchain networks decentralize:  • financial transactions  • digital ownership  • governance  • identity  • decentralized applications  The underlying philosophy remains the same:  Remove unnecessary central points of trust.  𝗙𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗕𝗶𝘁𝗧𝗼𝗿𝗿𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗧𝗼 𝗕𝗧𝗙𝗦  That evolution continues with the BitTorrent File System (BTFS).  BTFS applies peer-to-peer principles to decentralized storage by distributing encrypted file fragments across independent storage nodes rather than relying on a single cloud provider.  Files are identified using cryptographic hashes, while multiple replicas improve durability and availability.  Storage providers are incentivized through BTT, encouraging them to contribute storage space and maintain reliable uptime.  @BitTorrent @justinsuntron #TRONEcoStar

I completely agree. The next systemic risk in the banking sector is likely to stem not from defaults, but from a large-scale cyberattack.   Digitalization has greatly expanded the attack surface, and a serious incident affecting payment systems or critical infrastructure could have a faster and more profound impact than a traditional credit crisis. This risk continues to be greatly underestimated.

Cyberattack on NAIC, the U.S. state insurance regulators’ coordinating body. PeopleSoft zero-day was exploited - an unauthenticated SSRF-to-RCE chain. Apparently they had a working exploit before Oracle’s public advisory and patch. Attackers published stolen data and claim 3.1 TB was stolen, but NAIC hasn’t confirmed that scope. They also amended earlier dump claims blaming an “AI-generated misinterpretation.” These days, even cybercriminal activities are affected by AI hallucinations?

做 AI 想搞钱、又不想从零造概念的看这条:今天 GitHub 涨得最猛的一批,几乎全是"给 AI 配料"的——记忆、品味、技能包、官方插件。模型谁都追不动,但给模型铺路的活,一个人就能接。我挑了 7 个正在涨的,每个说清是什么、强在哪、你能顺着它做个什么。  1/ Understand-Anything(Egonex-AI/Understand-Anything)⭐6.9万 今日+4,697。这条赢在"势头"——今天涨得最猛的就是它。它把任何一坨代码变成一张能探索、能搜索、能直接提问的交互式知识图谱,Claude Code、Cursor、Copilot、Gemini CLI 都接得上。所有人都在用 AI 写代码,但一进大项目就抓瞎:AI 读得了单文件,讲不清整个仓库怎么咬合。它把这道坎做成了能看的图。顺着它做个什么:做一个托管版,挂到团队的代码库上、每次 PR 自动把图谱刷新,卖"新人一天上手老项目";或者针对某个流行框架/某个行业的祖传系统做专版,把"读懂这套代码"打包成订阅。github.com/Egonex-AI/Underst…  2/ taste-skill(Leonxlnx/taste-skill)⭐5.3万 今日+1,430。这条胜在"传播力"——它戳的是所有人都受够了的那件事:AI 写出来的东西又平又套。它是一个给 AI"装品味"的 skill,专治那种一眼假的通用废话。skill 这种形态本身就传得快、还好改。顺着它做个什么:做"品牌嗓音"包——把某个品牌、某个行业的腔调(法律但有人味、奢侈品、开发者那种冷幽默)调成一个专属 taste skill 卖;或者干脆做一个品味档案的市场,谁都能挑一套自己想要的调性装上。github.com/Leonxlnx/taste-sk…  3/ claude-mem(thedotmack/claude-mem)⭐8.5万 今日+352。这条的看点是"护城河深"。它给 agent 装了跨会话的记忆:把这次干了什么自动抓下来、用 AI 压缩、下次再把相关的上下文喂回去,Claude Code、Codex、Gemini、Copilot 通吃。agent 最大的尴尬就是"每次都失忆",它把这事补上了。顺着它做个什么:做记忆即服务——托管一份、团队共享的记忆、一家公司的知识能跨人跨项目地攒下来。真正的护城河不是代码,是越用越厚的那份记忆数据,别人抄得走工具抄不走积累。github.com/thedotmack/claude…  4/ knowledge-work-plugins(anthropics/knowledge-work-plugins)⭐2.2万 今日+1,718。这条最值钱的是"卡位"——这是 Anthropic 官方刚开的、给知识工作者在 Claude Cowork 里用的插件仓库。官方亲自下场、地基刚浇,但只铺了基础几样,财务结账、法务收件、招聘初筛这些细分场景几乎是空地。顺着它做个什么:照着官方格式做一个"把某类知识工作做顺"的插件去卖——官方分发的口子刚开,谁先把一个垂直活做扎实,谁就吃第一波生态红利。这是今天这批里,给"想现在就搞钱"的人最实在的一个位子。github.com/anthropics/knowle…  5/ Anthropic-Cybersecurity-Skills(mukul975/Anthropic-Cybersecurity-Skills)⭐2.3万 今日+880。这条赢在"可建、还能照搬"。它是 754 个结构化的网络安全 skill,对齐了 MITRE ATT&CK、NIST 这些框架,走 agentskills.io 标准,20 多个平台通用。它证明了一件事:把一个受监管领域的专业知识,按一个公认标准做成 skill 包,是立得住的形态。顺着它做个什么:把同一套打法搬到另一个合规重的垂直——医疗 HIPAA、金融 SOC2、隐私 GDPR——做一套对齐标准的 skill 包,再配一个托管的自动审计,卖给那个圈子里没人手做合规的小团队。github.com/mukul975/Anthropi…  6/ twenty(twentyhq/twenty)⭐5.2万 今日+216。换个口味,这条不在 skill 圈:它是开源版的 Salesforce,而且是冲着 AI 重做的。顺着它做个什么——别去重造一个,坐在它上面做生意:给某个垂直(房产中介、代运营机构、诊所)配好一套开箱即用的 CRM 模板,或者做一个托管版,带"从 Salesforce 搬家"+ 上手陪跑。跟当年靠托管 WordPress、托管开源数据库赚钱是同一个套路:底层有人替你养,你卖上面那层"省心"。github.com/twentyhq/twenty  7/ OpenStock(Open-Dev-Society/OpenStock)⭐1.3万 今日+156。这条胜在"发散"——它是贵价行情平台的开源平替:实时价格、个性化提醒、公司洞察,永久免费。免费开源本身不赚钱,但它给你省掉了从零搭的成本。顺着它做个什么:托管它,然后把"便利"卖成订阅——更快的数据源、更聪明的提醒规则、组合分析;或者只服务一个细分(某类资产、某个市场)做深。开源做内核、收钱靠托管和省心,经典开放内核打法。github.com/Open-Dev-Society/…  只挑一个上手,我推 knowledge-work-plugins。理由是它踩在一个"刚开、又一定会变大"的位子上:官方亲自下场把知识工作的插件生态铺开了,需求正在涨(用 Cowork 的人都在等好插件),但细分场景几乎是空地。你不用造新概念、不用养模型,顺着官方的格式和分发,把某一个知识工作的活(对账、合同初审、招聘筛人随便一个)做扎实,就能接住第一波生态红利。今天这批里,它是离"现在就能收钱"最近的那个。

Clone voices, dictate, and generate audio locally — the free ElevenLabs alternative. github.com/jamiepine/voicebo…  3. ai-website-cloner-template (~22K stars) Clone any website with a single command using your AI coding agent. Great for rapid prototyping and learning. github.com/JCodesMore/ai-web…  4. ⚠️ New threat actor on the radar ⚠️  🥷🏻 Booba Project 🗓️ added on June 26, 2026  🥢 Overview Booba Project is a newly emerged ransomware/extortion operation with an extremely limited public footprint. As of today, the group appears to be in its early operational phase, with only a handful of publicly observed victims and very little technical reporting available.  ⚙️ Core modus operandi Current observations suggest a double-extortion model: - file encryption - data exfiltration - direct victim extortion - publication through a dedicated onion leak site  No public technical analysis of its encryptor, builder, or malware lineage has yet been released. There is currently no evidence linking it to a known RaaS ecosystem or leaked ransomware codebase.  😱 Notable breaches At present, only isolated victims have been publicly indexed.  The first publicly tracked victim belongs to the Food & Beverage sector in the United States (June 2026). No large campaign or sector specialization has yet emerged.  🧰 @MITRE ATT &CK status As of today: - no MITRE ATT&CK Group entry - no Mandiant UNC designation - no Microsoft Storm/DEV designation - no CrowdStrike actor mapping  The actor remains unclustered.  👉🏻 What to watch next (#SOCPlaybook cues) - additional victims to establish victimology consistency - malware samples for code lineage analysis (Babuk, LockBit, Conti, Phobos derivatives, etc.) - ransom note wording and negotiation workflow - onion infrastructure reuse across campaigns - cryptocurrency wallet reuse - affiliate recruitment activity on underground forums - overlaps in infrastructure with recently emerged ransomware brands  🤓 Analyst take (confidence: low) Booba Project currently appears to be an emerging ransomware brand rather than an established operation. The available evidence is insufficient to determine whether it represents: - a genuinely new threat actor, - a rebranding of an existing crew, or a small affiliate operating independently under a new name.  Until malware samples, infrastructure overlaps, or recurring TTPs become available, Booba Project should be considered an early-stage ransomware operation under observation, rather than a mature ransomware ecosystem.  #ransomNews #cybersecurity #newthreatactor

The attack on the Miyetti Allah Chairman appears to have been calculated to inflame tensions and potentially trigger another round of violence in the Okpokwu/Ohimini communities. At a time when Benue has enjoyed a measure of relative peace, any attempt to destabilize the fragile security situation should be condemned by all.  The swift response by the police, including the arrest of suspects, is a positive step that could help prevent reprisals and reassure affected communities. However, the government and security agencies must go beyond arrests by engaging community leaders, traditional rulers, and relevant stakeholders to de-escalate tensions and preserve the existing peace.  The Fulani community, like every other group, should resist any attempt to exploit this incident to provoke further violence. Everyone should allow law enforcement agencies to carry out a thorough and impartial investigation so that those responsible are brought to justice. Peace must remain the priority, and no individual or group should be allowed to drag Benue back into another cycle of conflict.

sample_texts: As someone actually working in security analysis, my take is completely different you missed the rea

sample_tweets.text: As someone actually working in security analysis, my take is completely different you missed the real  point. yes, AI models are getting better at finding vulnerabilities in code. yes, they're making app security teams more efficient. that part is real.  BUT here's what everyone's ignoring: the same AI tools attackers are using are getting just as capable. and unlike defenders who follow rules, attackers don't have guardrails. ransomware hit record highs in 2024 with 44% of all breaches involving ransomware and 75% of system-intrusion breaches. breach frequency isn't going down. it's accelerating.  state-sponsored attackers (China, Russia, North Korea), organized ransomware groups, and well-funded threat actors all have access to the same AI tools now. they're using them for reconnaissance, exploit generation, credential theft, and lateral movement. AI-driven attacks are growing faster than defenses can scale.  so here's the reality: app security getting more efficient? cool. but that's ONE part of the job. the real bottleneck isn't finding bugs in code. it's defending the entire attack surface. and that's exploding.  we need people in: • SOC teams (monitoring millions of alerts) • Identity and access management (blocking lateral movement) • Supply chain security (vendors are the weakest link) • Incident response (forensics, recovery, investigation) • Cloud security (misconfigurations are everywhere) • Threat intelligence (understanding what's coming) • Phishing and social engineering defense (humans are still the entry point) • DevSecOps (shifting security left in development)  DevSecOps market alone is projected to grow from $3.73B in 2024 to $41.66B by 2030   companies are most vulnerable because they're trying to patch everything at once. people are most vulnerable because attackers are using AI-generated phishing at scale. phishing emails generated by AI are indistinguishable from real ones, and 40% of breaches start with email . the shortage isn't in app developers and bug hunters and in red teaming. it's on the DEFENSE side.  we have way more people learning to code and build products than learning to defend them. so AI didn't kill cybersecurity jobs. it changed the game. offense got a turbo boost. defense is still manually triaging alerts. the real demand is for defenders, and we don't have nearly enough. SOC analysts, incident responders, threat hunters, security architects - these roles are wide open and understaffed. if you're learning cybersecurity in 2026, don't worry about AI replacing you with this cheap models. worry about choosing the right side of the equation.

Your Balance Updated. The Blockchain Never Did.  On AscendEX, That Gap Is Where a Lot of People's Money Currently Lives.  For weeks, AscendEX users have described the same sequence: They request a withdrawal, the balance leaves their account, and then nothing happens. No transaction hash, no on chain movement, no coins arriving.   The exchange's own help center promises a TXID within two hours. Some users say they have waited days, a few say more than a month, with funds frozen in an "initiating" state that never resolves.  On June 26, on chain investigator @zachxbt put a name to the unease.  He reviewed AscendEX's known hot wallets on Arkham and TRM and found them thin on the assets people actually deposit, ETH, USDT, USDC, SOL, and weighted instead toward illiquid small caps.  His read was measured and pointed at once, the exchange is likely facing liquidity issues, and no one should be depositing to it.  🔹What the Missing Hash Actually Means🔹  A withdrawal with no TXID is the tell.  Your balance is a number in the exchange's private database, the coins are a separate pool the exchange custodies.  When the two diverge, you are not waiting on the blockchain, you are waiting on the exchange's permission to move its own reserves.   ZachXBT was careful here, and so everyone should be.  Thin hot wallets are operational liquidity, not the whole balance sheet, and most of a healthy exchange sits in cold storage that is not publicly tagged.  👉 This is not proof of insolvency, it is proof of strain and of opacity.  The trouble is you cannot tell those apart from the outside, and no proof of reserves has been published since mid 2025.  🔹The Permission Layer, in Its Purest Form🔹  I keep returning to the control layer welded into modern infrastructure. Circle can freeze a stablecoin, a staking wrapper can gate your yield, a prediction market can resolve against you.  A centralized exchange is the original version of that idea, and the cleanest (or dirtiest?).  ➡️ You deposited a permissionless asset and received an IOU and a dashboard.  The withdrawal button is the off switch, and it was never yours.  The most decentralized money ever built, parked behind the most centralized chokepoint in the stack. Meanwhile the official account keeps posting fresh token listings. Business as usual on the timeline, initiating on the withdrawals.  🔹The Script the Industry Knows by Heart🔹  We have watched this sequence before.  Delays, generic support replies, users muted in the group, reserves questioned, then a clearer picture that is sometimes benign and sometimes a crater.  Mt Gox, FTX, and a row of mid tier names taught one lesson, and it was always about custody rather than the coin.  This may resolve cleanly. AscendEX survived a 78 million dollar Lazarus hack in 2021 and compensated users. It may also not.  The point is that you, the depositor, have no way to know, and that not knowing is the product you accepted the moment you handed over the keys.  🔹Actionable NFA for Wise Investors🔹  1️⃣ If your funds are on AscendEX, stop depositing, try small test withdrawals on the most liquid networks, document every ticket, and watch the tagged wallets on Arkham yourself.  Cancelling a stuck request has returned funds to spot balance for some.  2️⃣ Treat any "recovery service" that messages you as a second scam stacked on the first.  They always swarm these moments, and no honest party will DM you to retrieve your money.  3️⃣ The structural lesson outlives one exchange. Counterparty risk is not hedged by a bigger logo, it is hedged by self custody and by venues that publish live, verifiable reserves.   The assets you own on your wallet - with no withdrawal queue and no support ticket is the entire reason this technology exists.  A blockchain exists so a withdrawal needs no one's permission.  The moment it needs permission, you were never really on the blockchain... bro.

When AI Safety Becomes AI Feudalism  The danger is not only uncontrolled AI. It is controlled intelligence in the hands of the already powerful.  Start with what AI can actually do, because the case for worry only makes sense once you admit the case for hope. A model that reads a century of cancer literature in an afternoon. A tutor that never tires, available to a child in a village school with one shared laptop. Tools that help climate researchers model a coastline, help a disabled writer finish a book, help a junior doctor catch the symptom a tired human eye would miss. None of this is science fiction now. Some of it is already running in clinics and classrooms. That is the prize. And it is precisely because the prize is so large that the way we hand it out matters more than almost anything else in technology policy today.  Here is the worry, stated plainly. Over the past month, the strongest AI systems have started arriving in the world through a side door, and only certain people hold the key.  The shape of the new access regime On 26 June 2026, OpenAI released what it called its most capable model family to date, the GPT-5.6 line, including variants reported under the names Sol, Terra and Luna. It did not release these to the public. It released them, in a limited preview, to a small circle of what it termed “trusted partners”. Reporting put the number at roughly twenty organisations, among them Amazon through its Bedrock platform. OpenAI has said the restriction is temporary and not meant as a long-term default. It has not published the list of who is inside the circle. According to CNBC and others, the limited rollout followed a request from the United States government.  Sit with that sequence for a moment. The most powerful publicly announced model on Earth went first to a closed group, at the government’s request, and the rest of us were not told who that group contains.  This is not an isolated event. Days earlier, Anthropic suspended access to two of its own frontier models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, after a government directive citing national security. Foreign nationals were cut off, including the company’s own overseas staff. Access to Mythos 5 was then partly restored, but only to a vetted list of more than one hundred trusted US organisations, said to include Fortune 500 companies, critical-infrastructure operators and participants in a security programme called Project Glasswing.  And there is a legal scaffolding now sitting underneath all of this. On 2 June 2026, the White House issued an executive order titled “Promoting Advanced Artificial Intelligence Innovation and Security”. Among other things, it directs federal agencies to build a voluntary framework giving government early access to frontier models, with a window of up to thirty days before wider release, and it encourages developers and agencies to jointly select “trusted partners” for that early access. Read the law firm summaries from Skadden, WilmerHale and Wiley and the pattern is consistent. The state wants a first look. The state wants a say in who else gets one.  Put the three together and a structure comes into view. Frontier intelligence is increasingly released in tiers. Governments and defence-linked bodies near the top. Large, vetted corporations next. Then, eventually, the rest of us: the startups, the independent researchers, the small builders, the universities, the civil-society groups, the Global South. Waiting.  What does “trusted partner” actually mean? This is the phrase the whole question turns on, and it is doing an enormous amount of quiet work.  Trusted by whom? Trusted to do what? A company can be trusted not to leak model weights to a hostile state. That is a real and reasonable form of trust. It is also a narrow, technical kind. It tells you a firm has good security engineers and a tidy compliance department. It tells you nothing about whether that firm should be among the first handful of institutions on the planet to wield a civilisation-shaping tool.  Technical trust and moral trust are different currencies, and the danger is that we are being invited to treat the first as if it were the second. A bank with excellent cyber-defences may still use early model access to crush smaller competitors. A defence contractor with spotless data handling may still want capabilities the rest of us would vote against if anyone asked. The vetting we have been told about measures the wrong thing. It measures whether a partner is safe for the model. It does not ask whether the partner is safe for the public.  The scandal is not only that ordinary people must wait. The scandal is that we do not know who is not waiting.  The safety risks are real. That is not the argument against them. It would be dishonest to wave away the reasons offered for all this caution. Frontier models genuinely can lower the cost of writing malware, probing critical infrastructure, or assembling dangerous technical knowledge. A staged rollout, where a developer studies a model’s behaviour before throwing the doors open, is not inherently sinister. It can be responsible engineering. OpenAI’s own deployment-safety materials frame the GPT-5.6 preview in roughly those terms, and serious people inside these labs lose sleep over genuine threats.  OpenAI’s cyber-specific access programme makes the dilemma even clearer. Through its Daybreak initiative and Trusted Access for Cyber, the company is giving verified defenders access to more capable and more permissive cybersecurity assistance, including tools designed for vulnerability triage, malware analysis, reverse engineering, detection engineering and patch validation. This is exactly the kind of work AI should help with. The world’s digital infrastructure is fragile, under-defended and full of neglected vulnerabilities. If AI can help patch hospitals, schools, water systems, public software and open-source infrastructure, that is not a reason for fear. It is a reason for hope.  But it also sharpens the democratic question. More capable models, more permissive behaviour, more access for some, stricter limits for others. That may be justified in cybersecurity, where the same knowledge can defend a system or attack one. Yet it means “trusted access” is no longer a one-off exception around GPT-5.6. It is becoming a governance model. And once again the central question returns: who counts as a defender, who gets the permissive model, who decides, and by what public standard?  So the problem is not the existence of caution. The problem is what caution becomes when it is exercised in the dark, and when the hand on the gate also has interests of its own.  Safety, properly understood, is a service to the whole of humanity. It slows things down so that everyone is protected from the genuine downside. Capture wears the same clothes but serves a different master. It slows things down for most people while quietly speeding things up for a chosen few. From the outside, on a press release, the two can look identical. The only way to tell them apart is transparency, and transparency is exactly what is missing.  Real safety protects humanity. Captured safety protects the powerful. The difference between them is not the language used to justify the delay. It is whether you can see who benefits from it.  Why this is especially alarming now A neutral, rule-bound state administering early access through published criteria and independent review would be one thing. That is not the state we have.  Consider the one genuinely encouraging episode in this whole story. Anthropic, by multiple accounts, refused Pentagon demands to strip safeguards that block its models from being used for fully autonomous weapons and for mass surveillance of US citizens. For holding that line, the company was reportedly labelled a supply-chain risk and effectively blacklisted by the Department of Defense, a designation it is contesting. NPR and the Congressional Research Service both documented the dispute. Whatever you think of Anthropic, that is a firm declining to remove the brakes on the most frightening uses of its own technology, and being punished by the government for it.  Now hold that beside the access decisions. The same federal apparatus that pressed a company to enable autonomous targeting and domestic surveillance is the apparatus now helping decide which corporations get frontier intelligence first. That should give anyone pause, regardless of party.  It should give more pause given who currently runs that apparatus. This is an administration whose entanglements with private wealth have been documented at length by mainstream outlets and watchdogs. Reporting from the Brennan Center and others has tracked conflicts of interest and self-dealing across the executive branch. There has been sustained coverage of the Trump family’s crypto ventures and the regulatory favours that have flowed around them. I am not alleging that AI access has been sold. There is no evidence of that, and I will not pretend otherwise. The argument here is structural, not criminal.  A government already deeply entangled with billionaire and corporate power should not be quietly influencing which companies receive the most powerful intelligence ever built, behind a partner list the public is not allowed to read. You do not need to prove a single corrupt act for that arrangement to be dangerous. The danger is in the design. Opaque power plus enormous stakes plus undisclosed beneficiaries is a recipe that does not need bad men to go wrong, though it certainly helps the bad ones if they show up.  A society serious about AI safety would not only ask whether a model is dangerous. It would ask whether those receiving it first are dangerous.  This is really a question about who owns the future Step back and the AI access debate turns out to be an old argument in new clothing. Every transformative technology has faced the same fork. Printing, electricity, the early internet. Each could have been enclosed by whoever got there first, and each delivered most of its human value only when it spread. Intelligence is the most general-purpose capability of all. If it concentrates, everything downstream concentrates with it: economic advantage, scientific lead, political leverage, the ability to shape what people see and believe.  Enclosure of intelligence would not just be unfair. It would harden every existing inequality into something closer to permanent. The gap between the firm with the frontier model and the firm without it does not stay still. It compounds. A doctrine that lets the already powerful get there first, and then locks the gate behind them in the name of safety, is not protecting the public. It is building a new aristocracy and calling it caution.  What honest safety would look like None of this requires abandoning safety. It requires refusing to let “safety” become a password that only the powerful know. A defensible regime would rest on a few firm principles.  Publish the criteria. If there is a standard for becoming a trusted partner, it should be written down, public, and the same for everyone. Disclose the partners. The public has a right to know which institutions receive civilisation-shaping tools ahead of everyone else, even when the model itself stays restricted for genuine reasons. Build independent oversight. Decisions of this magnitude should not sit with a developer and a friendly agency alone, but answer to a body with real distance from both, with civil-society and academic seats at the table.  Protect civil liberties in hard terms. No model access should ever be conditioned on enabling mass domestic surveillance or autonomous lethal targeting, and the firms that refuse such uses should be rewarded, not blacklisted. Forbid secret corporate favouritism, especially where the government deciding access is financially or politically entwined with the companies receiving it. And guarantee a real path to access for the people who make this technology serve everyone: public-interest researchers, smaller builders, universities, and institutions across the Global South, not as an afterthought once the powerful have had their head start, but as a condition of the system being called safe at all.  The believers in AI should be the loudest voices here, not the quietest. If you think this technology can lift human life, then you cannot also be relaxed about it being handed first to governments, militaries and a private list of the well-connected. Safety that protects everyone is the whole point. Safety that protects the powerful from the rest of us is enclosure wearing a borrowed coat.  We were promised a tool for humanity. The least we can ask is to be told who is holding it.  Source notes The following are the principal sources behind the factual claims above. Readers are encouraged to verify them directly. Where I have distinguished confirmed facts from reported claims and from my own argument, that distinction is deliberate. Model”: nytimes.com/2026/06/26/techn…  Anthropic–Pentagon dispute over autonomous weapons and surveillance (confirmed reporting):  Anthropic, “Where things stand with the Department of War”: anthropic.com/news/where-sta…  NPR, “Pentagon labels AI company Anthropic a supply chain risk”: npr.org/2026/03/06/g-s1-1127…  Congressional Research Service, “Pentagon-Anthropic Dispute over Autonomous Weapon”: congress.gov/crs-product/IN1…  OpenAI cyber-specific trusted access programme:  OpenAI, “Daybreak: Tools for securing every organization in the world”:  openai.com/index/daybreak-se… OpenAI, “Scaling Trusted Access for Cyber with GPT-5.5 and GPT-5.5-Cyber”:  openai.com/index/gpt-5-5-wit…  OpenAI describes Trusted Access for Cyber as a governed access model for verified defenders, designed to support authorised defensive cybersecurity work while maintaining safeguards against malicious use. That is the positive case. But it also means that selected organisations receive more capable and more permissive cybersecurity assistance than others.
